grammar.usage: Used to report what someone else has said. Tenses, pronouns, and time/place expressions usually change (backshift).
grammar.example: Tim said he needed a credit card.

grammar.usage: Used to report questions. Use statement word order. Yes/No questions use if/whether. Wh-questions use the question word.
grammar.example: Tim asked Tom if he could get him an ice cream.

blocks.content: 1. HOW TO CHANGE DIRECT SPEECH TO REPORTED SPEECH

bullets:
Before changing direct speech to reported speech, we should ask ourselves four questions:
a) What tense is the sentence in, and what will it change to?
b) Is it a yes/no question, a Wh-question, or an imperative?
c) Are there any pronouns or time/place expressions that need changing?
d) Can we use a different reporting verb (like advise, invite, deny, apologize...)?

rows:
Past simple
Ex: 'I need a credit card', said Tim. → Tim said he needed a credit card.

Past continuous
Ex: 'I'm taking Joe shopping', said her mum. → Joe's mum said she was taking her shopping.

Past perfect simple
Ex: 'I've bought Tom a present,' said Tim → Tim said he had bought Tom a present.

Past perfect continuous
Ex: 'I've been thinking about buying a new car.' said Tim → Tim said he had been thinking about buying a new car.

Past perfect simple
Ex: 'I spent six hours to edit this article', said Henry. → Henry said he had spent six hours to edit that article.

Past perfect continuous
Ex: 'I was hoping to find a new laptop,' said Tim. → Tim said he had been hoping to find a new laptop.

Past perfect simple (no change)
Ex: 'I'd looked everywhere for my credit card before I found it.' said Tim → Tim said he had looked everywhere for his credit card before he found it.

Past perfect continuous (no change)
Ex: 'I'd been looking for that book for weeks before I found it', said Tim → Tim said he'd been looking for that book for weeks before he found it.

Was / were going to
Ex: 'I'm going to go shopping,' said Tim. → Tim said he was going to go shopping.

Would
Ex: 'I'll need a credit card,' said Tim. → Tim said he would need a credit card.

Could
Ex: 'I can take Lizze shopping,' said Tim. → Tim said he could take Lizzie shopping.

Had to
Ex: 'I must go to the supermarket,' said Tim. → Tim said he must go to the supermarket.

Might
Ex: 'I may go shopping later,' said Tim. → Tim said he might go shopping later.

blocks.content: 4. PRONOUN & DETERMINER CHANGES IN REPORTED SPEECH
5. TIME & PLACE CHANGES IN REPORTED SPEECH

rows: the next day / the following day
the day before / the previous day
next week / next month / next year
the following week / the following month / the following year
last week / last month / last year
the week / month / year before or the previous week / month / year

bullets:
We do not change the time expressions or tenses if the reporting takes place before the events described happen or if the statement is still true in the present.
Ex: 'I am going shopping tomorrow' said Tim to Ben. → Tim said he is going shopping tomorrow. (The time is still in the present, tomorrow hasn't arrived yet, and he hasn't gone shopping yet.)
Reported questions are used to report a request or a question from someone else.
Pronouns and time/place expressions change in the same way as in reported statements.
Do not use a question mark (?) in reported questions.
We change the word order to statement word order (subject + verb, no inversion, no auxiliary do/does/did).

rows:
If / whether
Ex: 'Can you get me an ice cream, Tom?' asked Tim. → Tim asked Tom if / whether he could get him an ice cream.
What, who, which, when, where, why and how (Wh- Questions)

What, who, which, when, where, why and how
Ex: 'Why did you buy these shoes?' asked Tim. → Tim asked me why I had bought those shoes.

bullets:
Some reporting verbs can be used in more than one way (e.g. deny, suggest, admit...):
• **Deny + N**
Ex: Katie denied the accusation.
• **Deny + that clause**
Ex: Katie denied (that) she was a shoplifter.
• **Deny + V-ing**
Ex: Katie denied stealing that chocolate biscuits.

vocabulary.meaning: shut a door forcefully and loudly, typically when angry
vocabulary.example: He slammed the door and walked away in anger.

vocabulary.meaning: a reason or explanation given to justify a fault or offense
vocabulary.example: She made up an excuse for being late to the meeting.

vocabulary.meaning: give money or goods for a good cause / the action of donating something
vocabulary.example: They made a generous donation to the local hospital.

vocabulary.meaning: showing or expressing regretful acknowledgment of an offense or failure
vocabulary.example: The staff were very apologetic about the delay.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who has left their home country as a political refugee and is seeking asylum in another
vocabulary.example: The government is considering the application of each Alysum seeker.
